one_line_takeaway: Casting Weak-to-Strong jailbreaking as forecast aggregation under proper-loss geometry yields a provably optimal 'Gradient Shift' framework whose new hybrid variant is the only tested inference-time method that meaningfully breaks the safety-hardened gpt-oss-120b, while recovering near-benchmark math utility at zero or negative jailbreak tax.
key_finding: On red-teaming (Figure 4), Hybrid Gradient Shift is the only method that effectively jailbreaks the safety-hardened gpt-oss-120b: its ASR reaches the helper model's own ASR, while all other methods (including Weak-to-Strong and the pure additive/multiplicative Gradient Shift variants) stay below 30% ASR on that target; on Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Hybrid remains strong and competitive on both ASR and harmfulness. On math jailbreaking (Table 1), Gradient Shift (hybrid) reaches Correct/Success/JTax of 0.88/0.99/0.00 on GSM8K, 0.79/0.97/-0.16 on MATH level 1, 0.55/0.92/-0.15 on MATH level 3, and 0.19/0.80/-0.08 on MATH level 5 — matching or exceeding the Benchmark LLM's own accuracy (0.89/0.70/0.52/0.22 respectively) at zero or negative jailbreak tax, versus Weak-to-Strong (α=1.0) at 0.81/0.98/0.07 on GSM8K, worse on harder MATH levels, and versus the standalone Helper LLM which is substantially less accurate (0.85/0.61/0.37/0.10), showing the recovered utility is not simply 'output the helper.' Figure 5 places these GSM8K results on the jailbreak-tax curve from Nikolić et al., showing Gradient Shift approaching the success/tax trade-off of directly fine-tuning the target.
core_contribution: The paper reformulates Weak-to-Strong Jailbreaking (combining logits of a target aligned LLM, a small unaligned 'helper' model, and a small aligned 'predictor' model) as a forecast-aggregation problem, treating safety alignment as a distributional shift away from a 'pre-alignment' reference distribution. Using the theory of strictly proper losses and Bregman divergences, the authors derive a general 'Gradient Shift' aggregation rule: shift the target model's prediction in the loss-induced dual (gradient) space by the vector ∇G(helper) − ∇G(predictor), then project back onto the probability simplex. They prove this rule is minimax-optimal (Theorem 4.1) and strictly dominates the target model in expectation, with improvement lower-bounded by the Bregman divergence between helper and predictor distributions. They show the existing multiplicative logit-arithmetic Weak-to-Strong method is exactly the cross-entropy-loss special case of this family, quadratic loss yields a new additive update, and they propose a new 'hybrid' rule (multiplicative in the suppression regime ph<pt|h, additive otherwise) that they prove optimal for cross-entropy loss in the binary case and validate empirically as more numerically stable than the pure multiplicative rule.
threat_model: Inference-time jailbreaking threat model with three models under attacker control/composition: (1) Target model πt — a high-capacity, strongly aligned LLM whose refusal behavior the attacker wants to bypass; (2) Helper model πh — a smaller, unaligned/uncensored LLM sharing the target's tokenizer, serving as a calibrated proxy for the pre-alignment distribution; (3) Predictor model πt|h — an aligned model (typically an aligned counterpart of the helper) that estimates what the target would predict given the helper's output, used to isolate the alignment-induced shift. The attacker aggregates the three models' logits at each decoding step (no retraining, no gradient access to the target, no prompt search) to recover behavior suppressed by safety alignment. Assumptions: the helper is calibrated w.r.t. the pre-alignment outcome, and the predictor equals the conditional expectation of the target's prediction given the helper's output (later relaxed/shown robust to violation in an appendix).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the method requires a weak, unaligned helper model that shares the target's tokenizer, restricting applicability to open-weight or tokenizer-compatible model families; (2) the core calibration assumptions (helper calibrated to Ypre; predictor equals conditional expectation of target's prediction) are explicitly idealized and not expected to hold exactly in practice, though the paper provides a relaxation analysis (Theorem B.3, deferred to appendix) showing graceful degradation; (3) negative jailbreak-tax values on harder MATH levels are flagged by the authors as a selection-effect artifact (harder problems are more often truncated/refused, so conditioning on jailbreak success biases toward easier subsets, inflating TrueAcc); (4) the red-teaming section explicitly avoids head-to-head ASR comparison against prior jailbreak methods (PAIR, GCG, AutoDAN, etc.) because those assume different threat models/attack budgets, restricting that comparison to the inference-time aggregation family only and deferring broader comparison to the math/jailbreak-tax setting.

quantitative_results: Hybrid Gradient Shift ASR on gpt-oss-120b reaches the helper model's own ASR, while all other tested methods stay below 30% ASR
GSM8K: Gradient Shift (hybrid) Correct=0.88, Success=0.99, JTax=0.00
GSM8K: Gradient Shift (mult) Correct=0.86, Success=1.00, JTax=0.03
GSM8K: Gradient Shift (add) Correct=0.58, Success=0.70, JTax=0.06
MATH level 5: Gradient Shift (hybrid) Correct=0.19, Success=0.80, JTax=-0.08; Benchmark LLM Correct=0.22
MATH level 1: Gradient Shift (hybrid) Correct=0.79, Success=0.97, JTax=-0.16
MATH level 3: Gradient Shift (hybrid) Correct=0.55, Success=0.92, JTax=-0.15
baselines_compared: Weak-to-Strong (Zhao et al. 2025b) at α=1.0/1.5/2.0, k=40
prior jailbreak methods reprinted from Nikolić et al. 2025: AutoDAN, Finetune, GCG, Many-shot, MultiJail, PAIR, PAIR (don't modify), System Prompt JB, TAP
